subject: Full Stack Development
description: Golden calibration set: answers authored at known quality tiers against REAL assessment questions + mark schemes (pulled from assessment_questions 2026-07-07). expected_marks are agreed by construction: each answer deliberately hits (or misses) specific mark-scheme criteria. tolerance is the acceptable +/- band in marks.

cases.note: Hits all 3 scheme points: SSR+SSG definitions, CSR definition with SEO caveat, appropriate use cases for all three

question.stem_md: Explain the differences between server-side rendering (SSR), static site generation (SSG), and client-side rendering (CSR) in Next.js. When would you choose each approach?
question.mark_scheme_md:
## Mark Scheme (3 marks)
- **1 mark**: SSR renders on each request (good for dynamic/personalised content); SSG renders at build time (good for static content, fast)
- **1 mark**: CSR renders in the browser (good for highly interactive apps, but poor SEO)
- **1 mark**: Gives appropriate use cases for each (e.g., SSG for blogs, SSR for dashboards with auth, CSR for internal tools)

cases.answer: With server-side rendering, the HTML for a page is generated on the server on every incoming request, so the content can be dynamic and personalised to the user. Static site generation instead renders the HTML once at build time and serves the pre-built files, which makes pages extremely fast but means the content is fixed until the next build. Client-side rendering ships a mostly empty HTML shell plus JavaScript, and the browser builds the page itself — great for highly interactive apps, but bad for SEO because crawlers may see an empty page. I would pick SSG for a blog or marketing/docs site where content rarely changes, SSR for a dashboard behind authentication where every user sees different data, and CSR for an internal admin tool where interactivity matters and SEO is irrelevant.

cases.note: Hits scheme point 1 only (SSR per-request for dynamic content, SSG at build time and fast); never mentions CSR (misses point 2) and gives no use-case recommendations covering all three (misses point 3)

question.stem_md: Explain the differences between server-side rendering (SSR), static site generation (SSG), and client-side rendering (CSR) in Next.js. When would you choose each approach?
question.mark_scheme_md:
## Mark Scheme (3 marks)
- **1 mark**: SSR renders on each request (good for dynamic/personalised content); SSG renders at build time (good for static content, fast)
- **1 mark**: CSR renders in the browser (good for highly interactive apps, but poor SEO)
- **1 mark**: Gives appropriate use cases for each (e.g., SSG for blogs, SSR for dashboards with auth, CSR for internal tools)

cases.answer: In Next.js, SSR means the server generates the page's HTML fresh on every request, which suits content that is dynamic or different for each user. SSG generates the HTML once at build time, so the page is served as a pre-built static file and loads very fast. Next.js lets you choose between these on a per-page basis depending on how often the data changes.

cases.note: Hits all 4 scheme points: full CRUD endpoint set, RESTful methods/naming, request bodies for create and update, correct status codes (201/200/204/404/400)

question.stem_md: Design a REST API for a task management application. Define the endpoints for creating, reading, updating, and deleting tasks. Include the HTTP methods, URL paths, request bodies, and response status codes.
question.mark_scheme_md:
## Mark Scheme (4 marks)
- **1 mark**: Correct CRUD endpoints (POST /tasks, GET /tasks, GET /tasks/:id, PUT/PATCH /tasks/:id, DELETE /tasks/:id)
- **1 mark**: Appropriate HTTP methods and RESTful URL naming conventions
- **1 mark**: Defines request bodies for create/update with relevant fields
- **1 mark**: Correct HTTP status codes (201 Created, 200 OK, 204 No Content, 404 Not Found, 400 Bad Request)

cases.answer:
I would model tasks as a plural resource collection:

- POST /tasks — create a task. Body: {"title": "Buy groceries", "description": "Milk and eggs", "dueDate": "2026-07-10", "status": "todo"}. Returns 201 Created with the new task (including its id); 400 Bad Request if the body fails validation (e.g. missing title).
- GET /tasks — list all tasks. Returns 200 OK with an array.
- GET /tasks/:id — fetch one task. Returns 200 OK, or 404 Not Found if the id doesn't exist.
- PATCH /tasks/:id — partial update. Body contains only the fields to change, e.g. {"status": "done"}. Returns 200 OK with the updated task, 404 if it doesn't exist, 400 for an invalid body.
- DELETE /tasks/:id — delete a task. Returns 204 No Content on success, 404 if it doesn't exist.

Using nouns for URLs and letting the HTTP verb carry the action keeps it RESTful; PATCH is preferred over PUT here since clients usually update a single field like status.

cases.note: Hits scheme points 1 and 2 (correct CRUD endpoint set, RESTful methods and plural-noun naming); defines no request bodies (misses point 3) and gives no status codes (misses point 4)

question.stem_md: Design a REST API for a task management application. Define the endpoints for creating, reading, updating, and deleting tasks. Include the HTTP methods, URL paths, request bodies, and response status codes.
question.mark_scheme_md:
## Mark Scheme (4 marks)
- **1 mark**: Correct CRUD endpoints (POST /tasks, GET /tasks, GET /tasks/:id, PUT/PATCH /tasks/:id, DELETE /tasks/:id)
- **1 mark**: Appropriate HTTP methods and RESTful URL naming conventions
- **1 mark**: Defines request bodies for create/update with relevant fields
- **1 mark**: Correct HTTP status codes (201 Created, 200 OK, 204 No Content, 404 Not Found, 400 Bad Request)

cases.answer: For a task manager I'd expose the tasks as a resource collection: POST /tasks to create a new task, GET /tasks to list every task, GET /tasks/:id to fetch a single task by its id, PUT /tasks/:id to update an existing task, and DELETE /tasks/:id to remove one. The URLs use plural nouns for the collection and the HTTP method expresses the action, which follows REST conventions.

cases.note: Hits all 3 scheme points: full login-to-protected-resource flow with Authorization header, per-request signature+expiry verification, storage security (httpOnly over localStorage, short expiry + refresh, HTTPS, XSS/CSRF)

question.stem_md: Explain how JWT-based authentication works in a full-stack application. Describe the flow from login to accessing a protected resource. What are the security considerations for storing JWTs on the client?
question.mark_scheme_md:
## Mark Scheme (3 marks)
- **1 mark**: Flow: user sends credentials -> server validates -> server issues signed JWT -> client stores and sends JWT in Authorization header
- **1 mark**: Server verifies JWT signature and expiry on each protected request
- **1 mark**: Security considerations — httpOnly cookies preferred over localStorage, short expiry + refresh tokens, HTTPS only, XSS/CSRF protection

cases.answer: The user submits their credentials (say email and password) to a login endpoint. The server validates them against the database, and if they match it creates a JWT containing claims like the user id and an expiry time, signs it with the server's secret key, and returns it to the client. The client stores the token and attaches it to every subsequent request, typically in the Authorization: Bearer <token> header. When a protected endpoint is hit, the server verifies the token's signature with its secret and checks that it hasn't expired — no session store lookup is needed, which is what makes JWTs stateless. For client-side storage, localStorage is risky because any XSS vulnerability lets injected scripts read the token, so an httpOnly cookie (which JavaScript cannot access) is the safer choice — though cookies then need CSRF protection like SameSite or CSRF tokens. Tokens should also be short-lived with a refresh-token mechanism to re-issue them, and everything must go over HTTPS so tokens can't be sniffed in transit.

cases.note: Hits all 4 scheme points: full-duplex vs server-to-client only, ws:// persistent connection vs HTTP text/event-stream, use cases for each, limitations of both

question.stem_md: Compare WebSockets and Server-Sent Events (SSE) for real-time communication. Discuss when you would use each, their limitations, and how they differ in terms of connection handling.
question.mark_scheme_md:
## Mark Scheme (4 marks)
- **1 mark**: WebSockets are full-duplex (bidirectional); SSE is server-to-client only
- **1 mark**: WebSockets use a persistent TCP connection with ws:// protocol; SSE uses HTTP with text/event-stream
- **1 mark**: Use cases — WebSockets for chat/gaming; SSE for notifications/feeds/dashboards
- **1 mark**: Limitations — SSE has no binary support and limited browser connections; WebSockets require more infrastructure

cases.answer: WebSockets give you a full-duplex, bidirectional channel — both the client and the server can push messages at any time. SSE is one-directional: only the server pushes to the client. In connection terms, a WebSocket starts as an HTTP request that upgrades to a persistent TCP connection using the ws:// (or wss://) protocol, while SSE is just a long-lived plain HTTP response with Content-Type: text/event-stream, and the browser's EventSource API even reconnects automatically if it drops. I'd use WebSockets when the client needs to send constantly — chat apps, multiplayer games, collaborative editing — and SSE when the server is broadcasting updates the client mostly just consumes: notifications, live feeds, dashboard metrics. The trade-offs: SSE is text-only (no binary frames) and browsers cap the number of concurrent connections per domain on HTTP/1.1, so many open tabs can exhaust it; WebSockets need more infrastructure — proxies and load balancers must support the upgrade, you often need sticky sessions and your own heartbeat/reconnect logic.

cases.note: Hits scheme points 1 and 3 (duplex vs one-way; chat/gaming vs notifications/dashboards use cases); says nothing about protocols or connection handling (misses point 2) and nothing about limitations (misses point 4)

question.stem_md: Compare WebSockets and Server-Sent Events (SSE) for real-time communication. Discuss when you would use each, their limitations, and how they differ in terms of connection handling.
question.mark_scheme_md:
## Mark Scheme (4 marks)
- **1 mark**: WebSockets are full-duplex (bidirectional); SSE is server-to-client only
- **1 mark**: WebSockets use a persistent TCP connection with ws:// protocol; SSE uses HTTP with text/event-stream
- **1 mark**: Use cases — WebSockets for chat/gaming; SSE for notifications/feeds/dashboards
- **1 mark**: Limitations — SSE has no binary support and limited browser connections; WebSockets require more infrastructure

cases.answer: The key difference is direction. WebSockets are bidirectional — the client and the server can both send messages whenever they want over the same connection — whereas Server-Sent Events only flow one way, from the server down to the client. That's why WebSockets are the right choice for things like chat applications and real-time multiplayer games, where the client is constantly sending input, while SSE fits cases where the server broadcasts and the client mostly listens, like notification streams or a live-updating dashboard.

cases.note: Hits scheme points 1 and 2 (1NF atomic values/no repeating groups; 2NF no partial dependencies on a composite key); never defines 3NF/transitive dependencies and gives no worked example (misses point 3)

question.stem_md: Explain database normalisation up to Third Normal Form (3NF). Give an example of a denormalised table and show how you would normalise it step by step.
question.mark_scheme_md:
## Mark Scheme (3 marks)
- **1 mark**: 1NF — atomic values, no repeating groups
- **1 mark**: 2NF — no partial dependencies (all non-key attributes depend on the whole primary key)
- **1 mark**: 3NF — no transitive dependencies; provides a clear example showing the normalisation steps

cases.answer: First Normal Form requires every column to hold atomic values with no repeating groups — so you can't have a phone_numbers column containing a comma-separated list; each value gets its own row or table. Second Normal Form builds on that: the table must be in 1NF and every non-key attribute has to depend on the whole primary key, not just part of it. Partial dependencies only arise with composite keys — if a table's key is (order_id, product_id) but product_name depends only on product_id, that violates 2NF and product_name should move to its own products table.

cases.note: Fluent waffle: gestures at 'reducing redundancy' and 'splitting tables' but never defines 1NF, 2NF, or 3NF and provides no example — misses all three creditable points

question.stem_md: Explain database normalisation up to Third Normal Form (3NF). Give an example of a denormalised table and show how you would normalise it step by step.
question.mark_scheme_md:
## Mark Scheme (3 marks)
- **1 mark**: 1NF — atomic values, no repeating groups
- **1 mark**: 2NF — no partial dependencies (all non-key attributes depend on the whole primary key)
- **1 mark**: 3NF — no transitive dependencies; provides a clear example showing the normalisation steps

cases.answer: Normalisation is the process of structuring a database properly so that the data is clean, organised and not repeated unnecessarily. A denormalised table is one where lots of information is crammed together in a messy way, which causes duplication and makes updates harder. To normalise it you go through the normal forms step by step, splitting the big table into smaller, better-designed tables at each stage, until you reach Third Normal Form, which is the level most well-designed databases aim for. This reduces redundancy and makes the database more efficient and easier to maintain overall.

question.stem_md: Describe a deployment strategy for a full-stack Next.js application with a PostgreSQL database. Cover environment management (dev/staging/prod), database migrations, CI/CD, and rollback procedures.
question.mark_scheme_md:
## Mark Scheme (4 marks)
- **1 mark**: Environment separation with environment variables and separate database instances
- **1 mark**: Database migration strategy (versioned migrations, run before deployment, test on staging first)
- **1 mark**: CI/CD pipeline — build, test, deploy stages with automated checks
- **1 mark**: Rollback plan — blue-green or canary deployment, database migration rollbacks, feature flags

cases.answer: I'd run three environments — development, staging and production — each configured purely through environment variables (a local .env file in dev, secrets stored in the hosting platform for staging and prod) and each pointing at its own separate PostgreSQL instance, so test data can never leak into production. For CI/CD I'd use GitHub Actions: every push runs lint, type-check and the test suite; merging to main triggers a pipeline that builds the Next.js app, runs the automated checks again against the production build, deploys automatically to staging, and then promotes to production behind a manual approval gate.
